description:
You are assisting a machine-learning engineer who is preparing a brand-new training workstation.  
Before any data can be copied to this host, the engineer wants a **complete, but NOT YET APPLIED**, firewall configuration to be handed over to the DevOps team.  
Your job is to create three artefacts inside a dedicated directory:

1. /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_rules.conf ‑ the raw iptables configuration that DevOps can later load with  
   the command:  
         iptables-restore < /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_rules.conf  

2. /home/user/ml_firewall/apply_firewall.sh ‑ a helper script that, when executed, will load the file above
   and then emit the word **SUCCESS** to stdout if the iptables-restore command exits with status 0.

3. /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_changes.log ‑ a permanent log that enumerates, in human-readable form,
   every rule contained in firewall_rules.conf.

Important functional requirements
---------------------------------
A. The directory /home/user/ml_firewall **must already exist** after you finish.  
B. The two files firewall_rules.conf and apply_firewall.sh must each be world-readable (chmod 644) so that
   DevOps can copy them without sudo.  
C. apply_firewall.sh must be executable for its owner (chmod 744).  
D. The entire solution must be created **without** invoking sudo; we are only preparing files.

iptables rule set to embed in firewall_rules.conf
-------------------------------------------------
Exactly the eight (8) rules below—no more, no less—must appear, in the precise **order** given.
The automated grader will compare your file byte-for-byte, so be careful with spacing.

1. *filter
2. :INPUT DROP [0:0]
3. :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
4. :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
5. -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
6. -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
7. -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
8. -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8000 -j ACCEPT
9. -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT
10. COMMIT

NOTE: Lines 1 and 10 are mandatory wrapper lines for iptables-restore, while lines 2-9 are the rules
themselves.  Keep every line exactly as shown; do not insert extra whitespace or trailing spaces.

apply_firewall.sh specification
-------------------------------
• Must start with the shebang line: #!/bin/bash  
• Must contain *exactly* two executable commands (excluding comments and blank lines):
     1. iptables-restore < /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_rules.conf
     2. echo "SUCCESS"
• Should include concise comment lines explaining what the script does.
• Must end with a single trailing newline.

firewall_changes.log specification
----------------------------------
The log must list each rule from firewall_rules.conf (lines 2–9 only, excluding *filter and COMMIT)
in the following JSON Lines (jsonl) format—one JSON object per physical line of the log:

{"timestamp":"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS","rule":"<verbatim rule text>"}

Formatting constraints:
• timestamp must be the time (local system clock) when you create the file, accurate to the second.  
  Use the same timestamp for every JSON object—you only need one consistent time snapshot, not per rule.  
• rule value must reproduce precisely the rule line from firewall_rules.conf, including all internal
  spaces but excluding leading/trailing whitespace.  
• There must be **exactly 8 JSON objects** (one for each rule line 2–9).  
• The file must terminate with a newline after the last JSON object.

What the automated grader will check
------------------------------------
1. Directory /home/user/ml_firewall exists.  
2. File permissions: firewall_rules.conf = 0644, apply_firewall.sh = 0744, firewall_changes.log = 0644.  
3. Byte-for-byte contents of firewall_rules.conf match the list above (including newlines).  
4. apply_firewall.sh meets the shebang requirement, contains exactly two commands, and ends with a newline.  
5. firewall_changes.log contains exactly 8 correctly-formatted JSON objects, correct timestamp format,
   accurate rule text, and ends with a newline.

Create the directory, craft all three files with the exact contents and permissions described, and you’re done.
truth:
Expected directory and files after successful completion:

Directory:
- /home/user/ml_firewall (permissions 755 — default mkdir)

Files and their required contents (each line shown with its terminating newline character; “\n” denotes newline):

1) /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_rules.conf  (permissions 644)
---------------------------------------------------------------
*filter\n
:INPUT DROP [0:0]\n
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]\n
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]\n
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT\n
-A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT\n
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT\n
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8000 -j ACCEPT\n
-A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT\n
COMMIT\n

2) /home/user/ml_firewall/apply_firewall.sh (permissions 744)
-------------------------------------------------------------
#!/bin/bash\n
# Load prepared firewall rules and confirm success\n
iptables-restore < /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_rules.conf\n
echo "SUCCESS"\n

3) /home/user/ml_firewall/firewall_changes.log (permissions 644)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Assuming the agent generates the file on 2024-05-01 at 14:07:30, the content must be (with newline after each object):

{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":":INPUT DROP [0:0]"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":":FORWARD DROP [0:0]"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":":OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":"-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":"-A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":"-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":"-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8000 -j ACCEPT"}\n
{"timestamp":"2024-05-01 14:07:30","rule":"-A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT"}\n

(Automated tests will accept any single, self-consistent timestamp in the correct format.)
